Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic this term. I'm 29, working part-time, and juggling a lot, so I couldn't stick to a perfect schedule. I usually carved out chunks of time on weekends and late evenings when I could focus without interruptions. I'd download everything I needed and then work through it in my own way, mostly reading and writing notes rather than watching videos endlessly. Some weeks I'd put in solid hours, other weeks barely anything until panic set in.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on deadlines and what else was happening in my life. I'd love to say I studied at regular times, but that would be a lie. When assignments were due, I'd cram hard the week before. Between deadlines, I'd sometimes go days without touching course materials. It wasn't ideal, but I've learned to work in bursts when my energy allows it.
Q3: Usually it was checking if something new had been posted or if there was an assignment deadline coming up. I'd log in maybe once a week just to scan announcements and download any new readings. Sometimes I'd realize I hadn't checked in a while and panic a bit, then log in to make sure I hadn't missed anything critical. It wasn't really about engagement—more about staying informed enough not to fall behind completely.
Q4: Not really, if I'm honest. The LMS never became a natural part of my routine. I'd use it when I needed something specific, but I didn't browse it casually or check it daily like some students might. My routine was more about working with downloaded materials offline. The mindfulness exercises I started doing actually helped me be more intentional about when I engaged, but the LMS itself stayed utilitarian for me.
Q5: The reading materials and written resources were most useful to me—that's just how I learn best. I could download PDFs, annotate them, and work through them at my own pace. The assignment guidelines were also essential because they told me exactly what was expected. I appreciated anything I could engage with through reading and writing rather than passive watching.
Q6: I mostly ignored the discussion forums and didn't watch many videos. The forums felt performative to me—like posting just to be seen rather than genuine learning. Videos required too much uninterrupted time, and I found I could get the same information faster by reading. I'd skim video transcripts if they were available instead of sitting through the whole thing.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to assessment or genuinely deepened my understanding of something I'd read. I'm at a point in life where I don't have time for busywork. If I could see how something would help me perform better on an exam or assignment, I'd engage with it. Otherwise, I'd prioritize ruthlessly.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. Required activities got done because they had to, even when I was exhausted or anxious. Optional ones? I'd think 'that looks interesting' and then never get to them because something else always felt more urgent. There's a guilt that comes with skipping optional content, but I had to make peace with not doing everything.
Q9: Absolutely—most of my actual studying happened offline. I'd download readings, print some out, and work through them with handwritten notes. I used external articles and textbooks from the library to supplement what was provided. My desk was covered in papers and notebooks, but none of that showed up in any system. The real learning happened away from the screen.
Q10: There were many times I'd spend hours working through downloaded materials, making detailed notes, and really wrestling with concepts—none of which the LMS would capture. I'd also discuss ideas with a friend who was taking similar courses, which helped me process material. My exam score reflects that I was learning, even when my login frequency didn't.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd access a PDF or reading once, download it, and then work with it for days offline. I might watch the first few minutes of a video to see if it was relevant, then never return to it online because I'd already decided how to use that time. One download could represent hours of subsequent study that happened entirely on paper.
Q12: Mid-semester was brutal. My anxiety was through the roof, and I felt completely overwhelmed by the number of courses I was managing. There were weeks where just opening the LMS felt like too much. I'd avoid it because seeing all the things I hadn't done made me feel worse. The mindfulness intervention actually helped me get through that period without completely falling apart.
Q13: Definitely. When I was fatigued or stressed, I'd do the bare minimum on the LMS—just enough to not miss deadlines. Competing responsibilities from work and life meant the course often got pushed aside until it became urgent. Confusion about expectations sometimes made me avoid tasks entirely because I didn't know where to start. The workload felt relentless at times.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd scroll through materials or have videos playing while my mind was completely elsewhere. I'd be too anxious or tired to actually absorb anything. I'd technically be 'engaging' with the LMS, but I wasn't learning. Those sessions felt hollow and frustrating because I knew I was wasting time I didn't have.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I should have, even knowing they were important. The anxiety made starting feel impossible sometimes—like if I couldn't do it perfectly, why start at all? I'd avoid tasks that felt ambiguous or overwhelming until the deadline forced my hand. It's not laziness; it's more like paralysis that comes with high stress and perfectionism.
Q16: Deadlines were everything. They were the main driver of my LMS activity. I'd have bursts of intense engagement right before something was due, then go quiet afterward. Without deadlines, I probably wouldn't have logged in at all some weeks. It's not the healthiest pattern, but it's how I functioned given everything else going on.
Q17: When I got good feedback, it motivated me to keep going and reassured me I was on the right track. But feedback often came too late to really change my approach for the next task. I appreciated detailed comments because they showed someone actually read my work. Generic feedback felt discouraging and made me question why I'd put in effort.
Q18: The course structure was both helpful and frustrating. Having clear modules helped me know what to focus on, but the sheer volume of content across 17 courses meant I had to pick and choose. I appreciated when expectations were explicit, but some weeks felt overloaded while others were sparse. More consistency would have helped me plan better.
Q19: Clear assignment rubrics made engagement easier because I knew what was expected. What made it harder was when readings weren't clearly prioritized—I couldn't tell what was essential versus supplementary. Also, having so many discussion requirements across courses made me disengage from forums entirely. I needed streamlined, focused content rather than overwhelming options.
Q20: I think that could happen when students are anxious and checking things repeatedly without actually working on submissions. Or they might be exploring, trying to figure out what's expected, but struggling to produce work. High activity doesn't mean productive activity—sometimes it's just stress manifesting as clicking around without direction.
Q21: That absolutely reflects my experience. My exam scores and assignment quality don't match my low LMS activity because I did most of my work offline. I'm a read-write learner, so I engage deeply with downloaded materials rather than clicking through online content. The LMS was just a delivery system for me, not where learning happened.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when students are active, what they access, how often they submit work. But they miss the depth of engagement entirely. They can't capture whether someone actually understood what they read or just scrolled past it. They miss offline study, discussions with peers, and the mental effort that goes into wrestling with difficult concepts.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely thinking about the material, connecting it to what I already know, and caring about understanding it deeply. It's not about time spent clicking or videos watched—it's about mental investment. For me, engagement looks like sitting with a printed reading, annotating it, and feeling something click. It's internal, not measurable by logins.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I struggled with anxiety this term, or how the mindfulness intervention genuinely helped me cope. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent with printed materials or conversations with friends about course concepts. It also wouldn't show my resilience—how I kept going despite feeling overwhelmed, bored, and stressed. That's the real story of my engagement.
